title: The Radical, Unclassifiable Art of William Blake
description:
in Art, Books, Poetry |   	July 17th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

William Blake is a house┬нhold name, or not far from it, but things get more co
body:
in Art, Books, Poetry |   	July 17th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

William Blake is a house┬нhold name, or not far from it, but things get more com┬нpli┬нcat┬нed when it comes to pro┬нfes┬нsion┬нal descrip┬нtion. He was a poet, a painter, and a print┬нmak┬нer, at least inso┬нfar as he wrote poet┬нry, paint┬нed paint┬нings, and made prints. But we canтАЩt hope to attain even a basic under┬нstand┬нing of his lega┬нcy if we regard him as one man who hap┬нpened to have the ener┬нgy to do three dif┬нfer┬нent things. In fact, the osten┬нsi┬нbly sep┬нa┬нrate artis┬нtic pur┬нsuits in which he engaged were but three aspects of a uni┬нfied act of cre┬нation, result┬нing in the likes of Songs of Inno┬нcence and of Expe┬нri┬нence┬аand his illu┬нmi┬нnat┬нed тАЬprophet┬нic booksтАЭ: unclas┬нsi┬нfi┬нable works by тАЬthe patron saint of unclas┬нsi┬нfi┬нable artists.тАЭ
So Evan Puschak, bet┬нter known as the Nerd┬нwriter, labels him in his new video above. Blake made books, тАЬwhich he designed, wrote, etched, col┬нored, and print┬нed him┬нself, using a tech┬нnique that he invent┬нed.тАЭ They тАЬmix and syn┬нthe┬нsize cat┬нe┬нgories, and as a result, the art┬нwork of the late sev┬нen┬нteen- and ear┬нly eigh┬нteen-hun┬нdreds did┬нnтАЩt real┬нly know what to make of them.тАЭ
It did┬нnтАЩt help that the Roy┬нal Acad┬нe┬нmy of Arts, found┬нed when Blake was in ado┬нles┬нcence, had laid down its own strict aes┬нthet┬нic, gener┬нic, and for┬нmal stan┬нdards. Offi┬нcial┬нly an engraver and accord┬нed the low┬нly sta┬нtus there┬нof, Blake devot┬нed his labors to real┬нiz┬нing his elab┬нo┬нrate┬нly idio┬нsyn┬нcrat┬нic visions using images and words togeth┬нer in ways that no artist had done before.
This choice тАЬto work as mak┬нer of words, mak┬нer of images, and cross┬нbreed┬нer of both, amount┬нed to a deci┬нsion to live in incom┬нmen┬нsu┬нrable neigh┬нbor┬нhoods of mean┬нing,тАЭ┬аwrites┬аThe Cam┬нbridge Com┬нpan┬нion to William Blake author Mor┬нris Eaves as quot┬нed by Puschak. Blake swam against the cur┬нrent тАЬof mod┬нern human under┬нstand┬нing, whose bedrock is the prin┬нci┬нple of spe┬нcial┬нiza┬нtion.тАЭ Today, near┬нly two cen┬нturies after his death, that prin┬нci┬нple still obtains, and in some ways more rigid┬нly than ever. But his work remains, from the Songs┬аand the prophet┬нic books to his illus┬нtra┬нtions of clas┬нsics like the┬аDivine Com┬нe┬нdy,┬аPar┬нadise Lost, and even the Bib┬нli┬нcal Book of Job, which he read as тАЬthe sto┬нry of a man who believes, mis┬нtak┬нen┬нly, that sal┬нva┬нtion requires a slav┬нish obe┬нdi┬нence to words writ┬нten in a book.тАЭ With his┬аrules, divi┬нsions, and cat┬нe┬нgories, man has cut┬аreal┬нi┬нty apart;┬аthrough his art, Blake sought to make it whole again.
Relat┬нed con┬нtent:
The Rad┬нi┬нcal Artis┬нtic & Philo┬нsoph┬нi┬нcal World of William Blake: A Short Intro┬нduc┬нtion
William BlakeтАЩs Hal┬нlu┬нci┬нna┬нto┬нry Illus┬нtra┬нtions of John MiltonтАЩs Par┬нadise Lost
The Oth┬нer┬нworld┬нly Art of William Blake: An Intro┬нduc┬нtion to the Vision┬нary Poet and Painter
William Blake: The Remark┬нable Print┬нing Process of the Eng┬нlish Poet, Artist & Vision┬нary
Enter an Archive of William BlakeтАЩs Fan┬нtas┬нti┬нcal тАЬIllu┬нmi┬нnat┬нed BooksтАЭ: The Images Are Sub┬нlime, and in High Res┬нo┬нlu┬нtion
William BlakeтАЩs 102 Illus┬нtra┬нtions of the Divine Com┬нe┬нdy Col┬нlect┬нed in a Beau┬нti┬нful Book from Taschen
Based in Seoul,┬аCol┬нin┬аMarshall┬аwrites and broad┬нcasts on cities, lan┬нguage, and cul┬нture. HeтАЩs the author of the newslet┬нter┬аBooks on Cities┬аas well as the books┬аэХЬъ╡н ьЪФьХ╜ ъ╕ИьзА┬а(No Sum┬нma┬нriz┬нing Korea) and┬аKore┬нan Newtro.┬аFol┬нlow him on the social net┬нwork for┬нmer┬нly known as Twit┬нter at┬а@colinmarshall.

title: A Walk Through HomerтАЩs Odyssey: A Guide to the Epic Before Seeing Christopher NolanтАЩs Film
description: YouтАЩre gear┬нing up to see Christo┬нpher NolanтАЩs Odyssey, but you havenтАЩt read the Home┬нr┬нic work. Or you read it so long ago that it feels lik
body:
YouтАЩre gear┬нing up to see Christo┬нpher NolanтАЩs Odyssey, but you havenтАЩt read the Home┬нr┬нic work. Or you read it so long ago that it feels like youтАЩve nev┬нer read it at all. No wor┬нries. Above, Tom Hol┬нland and Dominic Sand┬нbrook, the hosts of The Rest Is His┬нto┬нry pod┬нcast, take you through the major plot lines of the Odyssey, unpack┬нing the lit┬нer┬нary and his┬нtor┬нi┬нcal mean┬нing of the epicтАЩs dif┬нfer┬нent tales. ItтАЩs a good primerтАФjust what you need to get ready for one of the big┬нger cin┬нe┬нma releas┬нes this year. Enjoy!
If you would like to sup┬нport the mis┬нsion of Open Cul┬нture, con┬нsid┬нer mak┬нing┬аa dona┬нtion to our site. ItтАЩs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con┬нtri┬нbu┬нtions will help us con┬нtin┬нue pro┬нvid┬нing the best free cul┬нtur┬нal and edu┬нca┬нtion┬нal mate┬нri┬нals to learn┬нers every┬нwhere. You can con┬нtribute through Pay┬нPal, Patre┬нon, and Ven┬нmo (@openculture). Thanks!

title: Trompe-L'┼Уil Paintings by Jason Limon Reveal a Hidden Skeletal World
description: If you were to rip open a tattered matchbox, what might you find hidden in its confines? And whatтАЩs lurking behind biological renderings and adver
body:
If you were to rip open a tattered matchbox, what might you find hidden in its confines? And whatтАЩs lurking behind biological renderings and advertisements? Jason Limon imagines a playful world in which vintage illustrations are the colorful veneer concealing a vast, three-dimensional universe populated by skeletons.
The San Antonio-based artist has long painted otherworldly scenes dominated by life after death, when bony figures are stripped of their identities and instead function as anonymous entities. Tapping into emotion and personal experience, Limon continues to conjure the uncanny through a cheeky approach to one of the most universal symbols.
тАЬMatchbook Tiger,тАЭ 12 x 9 inches
In his most recent works blending acrylic and oil paints, the artist homes in on illusion, opting for a trompe-lтАЩ┼Уil technique. Many feature a torn element, as if paper has been ripped away to reveal an otherwise invisible world beneath. While Limon incorporated this effect in previous works, itтАЩs propelled his practice as of late because it offers more space for experimentation.
тАЬIt took a bit to press the brakes on how I work, but I intentionally decided to slow the pace of how I create the art while also trying to keep a little bit of playfulness,тАЭ he says. тАЬAs I play with painting in the background, it has taught me to loosen up in a variety of ways. At some point in the future, the two identities will creatively and visually merge as one.тАЭ
Originals and prints are available in LimonтАЩs shop, and you can follow his practice on Instagram.
тАЬCircus Clown,тАЭ 11 x 14 inches
тАЬPerle des Indes,тАЭ 11 x 14 inches

тАЬSeen,тАЭ 8 x 8 inches

тАЬFading Feline,тАЭ 12 x 12 inches
тАЬMatchbox Cyclist,тАЭ 9 x 12 inches
тАЬHush,тАЭ 10 x 10 inches

title: 'Voices in the Mirror' Honors the Legacy of Pioneering Photographer Gordon Parks
description: From portraits taken around Harlem in the 1940s to assignments for Life magazine to the 1963 March on Washington, Gordon Parks (1912-2006) wielded
body:
From portraits taken around Harlem in the 1940s to assignments for Life magazine to the 1963 March on Washington, Gordon Parks (1912-2006) wielded his camera as a tool for social justice. He captured civil rights activists like Malcom X and Martin Luther King, Jr. in addition to artists and celebrities such as Helen Frankenthaler and Ingrid Bergman. But he may be best known for his candid portraits of families and communities in the segregated South during the era of Jim Crow. All of these and more will be on view in Voices in the Mirror at Jack Shainman Gallery in mid-September, also marking the 20th anniversary of The Gordon Parks Foundation.
Parks was spurred to pursue photography in 1937 after seeing photos taken for the Farm Security Administration (FSA), which held a mission to document American life. тАЬI┬аsaw that the camera could be a weapon against poverty, against racism, against all sorts of social wrongs,тАЭ he said. тАЬI knew at that point I had to have a camera.тАЭ In 1942, he became the first Black photographer hired as part of the initiative, introducing him to Washington, D.C., where he noted that тАЬdiscrimination and bigotry were worse there than any place I had yet seen.тАЭ
тАЬMartin Luther King, Jr., Washington, D.C.тАЭ (1963), gelatin silver print, 16 x 20 inches 
Among the images included in Voices in the Mirror are seminal portraits like тАЬAmerican Gothic, Washington, D.C.тАЭ (1942), which captures a government worker named Ella Watson with a broom and mop. The work nods to American Regionalist painter Grant WoodтАЩs likewise iconic тАЬAmerican GothicтАЭ painting, created 12 years earlier as an ode to American values. ParksтАЩ image represented a starkly contrasted reality.
After speaking with Watson about her life and experience in D.C., Parks recalled that it was тАЬso disastrous┬аthat I felt that I must photograph this woman in a way that would make me feelтАФor make the public feelтАФabout what Washington, D.C., was in 1942.тАЭ He positioned her in front of a flag with a symbolic mop and broom. тАЬI didnтАЩt care about what anybody┬аelse felt,тАЭ he said. тАЬThatтАЩs what I felt about America and Ella WatsonтАЩs position inside America.тАЭ
The exhibition is accompanied by numerous anecdotes and reflections by some of those who appeared in the photos or had close relationships with those who did, such as Malcolm XтАЩs daughter, Qubilah Shabazz, and Cora Taylor, who was one of the women standing near a pair of segregated water fountains in тАЬSegregation in the SouthтАЭ (1956).
Voices in the Mirror opens on September 18 and continues through November 7 in New York. You might also be interested in the works of other FSA photographers who documented the South during the 1930s and 1940s, such as Russell Lee and Marion Post Wolcott.
тАЬAmerican Gothic, Washington D.C.тАЭ (1942), gelatin silver print, 16 x 20 inches
тАЬAt Segregated Drinking Fountain, Mobile, AlabamaтАЭ (1956), archival pigment print, 34 x 34 inches
тАЬUntitled, Shady Grove, AlabamaтАЭ (1956), archival pigment print, 48 x 48 inches
тАЬUntitled, Harlem, New YorkтАЭ (1963), gelatin silver print, 16 x 20 inches 
тАЬUntitled, AlabamaтАЭ (1956), archival pigment print, 42 x 42 inches
тАЬWoman and Dog in Window, Harlem, New YorkтАЭ (1943), gelatin silver print, 34 x 34 inches 
тАЬMr. and Mrs. Albert Thornton, Mobile, Alabama, 1956 (1956), archival pigment print, 50 x 50 inches
тАЬStore Front, Mobile, AlabamaтАЭ (1956), archival pigment print, 48 x 48 inches

title: Cocoa Plantations Set the Scene for Divine Events in Marc Padeu's 'Memento Vivere'
description: тАЬItтАЩs not that we have a short time to live, but that we waste much of it,тАЭ wrote stoic philosopher Lucius Seneca. The phrase appeared in his essa
body:
тАЬItтАЩs not that we have a short time to live, but that we waste much of it,тАЭ wrote stoic philosopher Lucius Seneca. The phrase appeared in his essay тАЬDe Brevitate Vitae,тАЭ or, тАЬOn the Shortness of Life,тАЭ which he scratched into papyrus around 49 A.D. Nearly 2,000 years on, his words reflect what is still a fundamental concern of lifeтАФhow to spend it wisely? For artist Marc Padeu, the notion of humansтАЩ futile control of time forms the basis of a new suite of works in Memento Vivere, on view starting tomorrow at Larkin Durey.
Padeu is known for merging scenes of daily life with references to Renaissance religious paintings. Among his newest works, тАЬLa promesse et lтАЩagneauтАЭ (тАЬThe promise and the lambтАЭ) most distinctly continues this theme. In the center of the composition, a young child is seated on the lap of his mother, both of whom are flanked by adults who bring gifts and tidings in a nod to the artistic tradition of the Adoration of the Magi, or the nativity.
Detail of тАЬLa promesse et lтАЩagneauтАЭ
Latin for тАЬremember to live,тАЭ the title nods to Seneca and serves as a reminder to grasp life by the reins before itтАЩs too late. ItтАЩs also a complement to more common art historical theme of memento mori, or тАЬremember you will die,тАЭ which serves a similar purpose of reminding the viewer that life is short and any wealth or grandeur one pursues means nothing when one is dead. Instead, itтАЩs important to focus on things that really matter: family, nature, craft, and so on.
PadeuтАЩs narrative portray individuals and communities who work on the cocoa plantations in his native Cameroon. тАЬDays follow one another, harvests come round again and yet, despite this ebb and flow of life, his figures are caught outside of time, slipping between past, present and future,тАЭ the gallery says. The artist infuses his compositions with a sense of divine birth and the mysterious sublime. тАЬEach painting is poised between promise and fate, light and shade; the figures existing simultaneously in a spiritual and secular realm, neither saints nor heroes but with a growing awareness that life is a fragile gift.тАЭ
Memento Vivere opens on July 17 and continues through August 14 in London. See more on the artistтАЩs Instagram.
тАЬSous le poids de la coupeтАЭ (2026), acrylic on canvas, 66 7/8 x 78 3/4 inches
тАЬMemento vivereтАЭ (2026), acrylic on canvas, 66 7/8 x 78 3/4 inches
Detail of тАЬMemento vivereтАЭ 
тАЬLa prix de la promesseтАЭ (2026), acrylic on canvas, 59 x 78 3/4 inches
тАЬLe chemin partag├йтАЭ (2026), acrylic on canvas, 70 x 47 1/4 inches

title: Four Centuries Later, We Still Don't Get Caravaggio
description:
Film Review
								A British docudrama serves as a salutary primer on the Baroque masterтАЩs greatest works, but we should be wary of what
body:
Film Review
								A British docudrama serves as a salutary primer on the Baroque masterтАЩs greatest works, but we should be wary of what it tells us about the personality of its subject.
						
								
																
														
								
						
		
	  		    
        
            Still from Caravaggio (2025), dir. David Bickerstaff and Phil Grabsky, with Jack Bannell as Caravaggio (image courtesy Seventh Art Productions)
    
		
				
						The fascination with Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio continues unabated, and for good reason. Not only was Caravaggio the most impressive Italian painter since the death of his namesake, Michelangelo Buonarroti, less than a decade before his birth, but his reputation as the тАЬdamned artistтАЭ lends itself to questions about his character, sexuality, politics, and professional practice.Today, we have a tendency to see him as a solitary, bellicose figure, and Caravaggio (2025), a documentary drama directed by David Bickerstaff and Phil Grabsky, does nothing to dissuade us, even though the story it tells is one of constant collaboration. Among the experts telling CaravaggioтАЩs story are the brilliant art historian Helen Langdon; the head of ChristieтАЩs research and expertise department, Letizia Treves; art historian Fabio Scaletti, to give an Italian perspective; and the artist Stephen Nelson. The film serves as a salutary primer, full of long pans up and down his most famous works so we can see up close what is normally kept well away from us in churches and museums, but we should be wary of what it tells us about the personality of its subject.

												Review

title: The ICA at VCU Brings Immersive Installation "Deo Vindice" to Richmond
description:
Announcement
                The gallery-sized installation at the Institute for Contemporary Art combines Greek mythology with Civil
body:
Announcement
                The gallery-sized installation at the Institute for Contemporary Art combines Greek mythology with Civil War history to examine white supremacy and hegemonic power.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Abigail DeVille,┬аDeo Vindice (OrionтАЩs Cabinet), 2025, china cabinets, charcoal, rusted steel scaffolding, pig blood, salt, mud, lights, and natural fiber, installation view,┬аAbigail DeVille: Deo Vindice (OrionтАЩs Cabinet),┬аICA at VCU, 2026. (artwork ┬й Abigail DeVille; photograph by David Hale)
    
        
            The Institute for Contemporary Art (ICA) at Virginia Commonwealth University (VCU) presents the Southeastern debut of Abigail DeVilleтАЩs Deo Vindice (OrionтАЩs Cabinet) (2025), an immersive, gallery-sized installation that recalls the burning of Richmond in 1865.On view through August 18, Deo Vindice is a ┬аsweeping assemblage of charred Colonial-style cabinets inspired by photographs of Richmond set ablaze in the last days of the Civil War. The installation was commissioned for the critically acclaimed MONUMENTS exhibition at the Museum of Contemporary Art, Los Angeles, in collaboration with The Brick.┬атАЬIn the MONUMENTS exhibition, Deo Vindice was situated among decommissioned Confederate statuary and other artworks that interrogate the unresolved legacies of Lost Cause revisionism and myth-making,тАЭ notes exhibition curator Amber Esseiva, Senior Curator and Director of Curatorial Affairs at the ICA. тАЬPresenting the work here in Richmond allows us to continue that essential inquiry into power, erasure, and public memory in the historically charged context our location provides.тАЭWhen it became clear that Union forces would capture Richmond in April 1865, the capital and industrial center of the Confederacy, Southern officials ordered their men to burn the cityтАЩs warehouses in order to prevent the munitions and railway foundry Tredegar Iron Works from falling into Union hands. Less than a week later, Robert E. Lee would surrender to Ulysses S. Grant at Appomattox, marking the end of the war.Curio cabinets have historically served as status symbols, especially in the South, protecting and displaying a familyтАЩs heirlooms. In the exhibition, DeVille arranges the cabinets in clusters to mark the primary stars of the constellation Orion, a celestial system named for the hunter from Greek mythology who enraged the Earth goddess Gaia by vowing to kill every animal on earth.┬а In doing so, she draws a comparison between OrionтАЩs will to dominate and that of the Confederacy to maintain white supremacy and hegemonic power, even to the point of destroying their own communities.┬аThe ragged cloth-draped scaffolding that connects the cabinets is of the type used in monument restoration, suggesting forms that have sheltered and enshrined distorted versions of historical memory, ensuring their survival despite the decades of scholarship disproving the myths.Deo Vindice (OrionтАЩs Cabinet) is curated by Amber Esseiva, Senior Curator and Director of Curatorial Affairs at the ICA, with Kennedy Jones, Curatorial Assistant. The installation is on view through August 18, 2026.┬аFor more information, visit icavcu.org.
                  
                      
                        Announcement
                      
                      
                        Exhibition Announcement
                      
                      
                        Richmond
                      
                      
                        Virginia
